updates.title: Claude Reflect Launches in Beta — Usage Dashboard with Monthly Recap and Focus Settings
updates.summary: Anthropic launched Claude Reflect in beta on July 9, a built-in dashboard found at Settings > Reflect that visualises how users engage with Claude. The monthly recap shows topics covered, most active day and peak hour, and observations framed around Anthropic's AI Fluency Framework (delegation, description, discernment, diligence). A companion Settings > Time and focus lets users set break reminders and quiet hours; it also surfaces reflective questions such as 'What's one thing you want to keep doing yourself, even if Claude could do it faster?' The feature is available on Free, Pro, and Max plans on Claude.ai and Claude Desktop and requires memory to be enabled; incognito chats and health-integration conversations are excluded from reports.
updates.accuracy_notes: Confirmed by TechCrunch (July 9), MacRumors (July 9), Eastern Herald (July 10), Digital Trends, and Testing Catalog — all citing the Anthropic release notes directly. No accuracy concerns; feature is live and verifiable in-app.

updates.title: Claude Cowork Expands to Web and Mobile with Cross-Device Sync and Background Processing
updates.summary: Anthropic rolled out Claude Cowork to web, iOS, and Android on July 7, extending the previously desktop-only agentic workspace to all devices. Key new capabilities include cross-device session sync (start a task on desktop, monitor on mobile), true background processing with no device online required, and a unified interface that merges chat, projects, and artifacts into one sidebar. The beta rollout starts with Max plan subscribers and gradually expands. Anthropic also released usage data from 1.2 million anonymised Cowork sessions showing business process operations (reconciling spreadsheets, onboarding checklists, report consolidation) as the largest use category at 33.4%. Usage limits for Cowork are doubled through August 5 to mark the launch.
updates.accuracy_notes: Confirmed by TechCrunch (July 7), VentureBeat (July 7), 9to5Mac (July 7), Android Authority, Android Headlines, and PYMNTS. Usage statistics sourced from Anthropic's own release data. High confidence.

updates.title: Microsoft 365 Connector Now Supports Write Tools — Draft Email, Manage Calendar, Edit OneDrive Files
updates.summary: The Microsoft 365 connector for Claude has been upgraded with write-tool support. Claude can now draft, send, and organise email; create and update calendar events; modify mailbox settings; and create or update files in OneDrive and SharePoint. This expands the connector from read-only context retrieval to full bi-directional productivity automation within Microsoft's ecosystem.
updates.accuracy_notes: Confirmed via Anthropic release notes (cited in multiple Releasebot summaries and the July 9 daily newsletter). The feature is gated by the Microsoft 365 connector being enabled. No contradictory reports found.

updates.title: I Used Claude Fable 5 for Zero-Shot Coding, and Understood Why Anthropic Locked It Down
updates.summary: XDA's hands-on evaluation of Claude Fable 5 — tested via zero-shot coding (building an original 2D game in Python using Pygame) after the model's turbulent timeline: public release June 9, US Dept. of Commerce withdrawal June 12 over export control concerns, and redeployment July 1. The review finds that the redeployed version performs materially closer to Claude Opus 4.8 than launch-day coverage implied, consistent with the hypothesis that Anthropic tightened safety controls during the model's three-week absence. The article concludes that Fable 5 in its current form is impressive but not the dramatic capability jump early benchmarks suggested.
updates.accuracy_notes: Well-researched hands-on test. The author's observation about post-return capability differences is consistent with independent reports and aligns with Anthropic's public acknowledgment of safety adjustments. The Fable 5 timeline (June 9 release, June 12 withdrawal, July 1 redeployment) is independently verified across multiple outlets.

updates.title: I Automated My Admin Tasks with Claude Code and Cowork — Here's Which One Actually Stuck
updates.summary: XDA hands-on comparison of Claude Code and Claude Cowork for administrative task automation, timed to coincide with Cowork's mobile and web expansion. The author finds Cowork better suited for folder-level, pattern-based admin tasks (tracking article pitches in a markdown table, auto-updating project files via conversation), while Claude Code — essentially Claude with direct folder access — offers more flexibility for custom scripting and edge-case handling. The headline finding: Cowork is the tool that 'stuck' for daily admin, while Claude Code remains preferred for complex or code-adjacent automation.
updates.accuracy_notes: Opinion and how-to piece based on first-person use. Claims are consistent with known capabilities of both tools. No extraordinary technical claims; standard product comparison. Cowork's July 7 expansion context is accurate.
